  • Hide All Code

Predicting BMW Stock Price Direction Using Machine Learning

Xinyi Yang

2026-05-11

pkgs <- c("tidyverse", "tidymodels", "discrim", "kknn",
          "vip", "patchwork", "lubridate", "ranger",
          "corrplot", "glmnet", "MASS")
install.packages(setdiff(pkgs, rownames(installed.packages())))

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(discrim)
library(kknn)
library(vip)
library(patchwork)
library(lubridate)
library(ranger)
library(glmnet)
library(corrplot)

tidymodels_prefer()
set.seed(131)

1 Introduction

1.1 Background and Motivation

Bayerische Motoren Werke AG (BMW) is one of the world’s leading premium automobile and motorcycle manufacturers. Its stock (ticker: BMW.DE) has been traded on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange since the 1960s. Predicting the short-term direction of stock prices is a classic and challenging problem in quantitative finance — one that has attracted enormous interest from both academic researchers and industry practitioners.

This project asks a focused, practically relevant question:

Can we predict whether BMW’s stock price will rise (go up) or fall (go down) on the next trading day, using only information available at the close of the current trading day?

This is framed as a binary classification problem. The outcome variable Target equals 1 if the next-day closing price is higher than the current day’s closing price, and 0 otherwise.

1.2 Dataset Description

The dataset is the BMW Stock Advanced Features dataset obtained from Kaggle. After removing any incomplete rows, 7,390 trading-day observations remain, each representing one trading day for BMW AG stock (ticker: BMW.DE) on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange.

Formal citation:

Sumon, Md Mahfuz. (2024). BMW Car with Advanced Features [Data set]. Kaggle. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/mdmahfuzsumon/bmw-car-with-advanced-features

Each row contains 31 features grouped as follows:

Group Variables
Price Open, High, Low, Close, Adj Close
Returns Return, Log_Return
Momentum Momentum_7, Momentum_30
Moving averages MA7, MA30, MA100, EMA20, EMA50
Volatility Volatility_7, Volatility_30
Spread / Range High_Low_Spread, Open_Close_Spread, Price_Range_Pct
Lagged prices Lag1, Lag2, Lag3
Time Year, Month, Day, DayOfWeek
Volume Volume, Volume_Change, Volume_MA7
Outcome Target (1 = next day up, 0 = next day down)

1.3 Why Classification? Why This Metric?

Stock return prediction is notoriously difficult because financial markets are close to efficient: publicly available information is rapidly priced in, leaving little systematic predictability. Rather than trying to predict the exact magnitude of tomorrow’s return (regression), we focus on its direction (classification), which is a simpler and more actionable goal.

Following the instructor’s recommendation to adopt a more informative evaluation metric, we use ROC-AUC (area under the receiver operating characteristic curve) as the primary performance metric throughout this project. ROC-AUC is preferred over accuracy because:

  1. It is threshold-independent: it measures discrimination ability across all possible decision thresholds, rather than fixing a cutoff at 0.5.
  2. It is robust to class imbalance: even though our classes are nearly balanced (50.8% down, 49.2% up), accuracy can be misleading when one class is easier to predict than the other.
  3. It has a natural probabilistic interpretation: ROC-AUC equals the probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen positive example higher than a randomly chosen negative example.

An ROC-AUC of 0.5 corresponds to random guessing; 1.0 is a perfect classifier.

2 Exploratory Data Analysis

bmw <- read_csv("bmw_advanced_features.csv") %>%
  mutate(
    Target     = factor(Target, levels = c("1", "0"),
                        labels = c("Up", "Down")),
    Date       = as.Date(Date),
    DayOfWeek  = factor(DayOfWeek,
                        labels = c("Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu","Fri"))
  )

cat("Observations:", nrow(bmw), "\n")
## Observations: 7390
cat("Features:    ", ncol(bmw), "\n")
## Features:     31
cat("Date range:  ", format(min(bmw$Date)), "to", format(max(bmw$Date)), "\n")
## Date range:   1997-03-27 to 2026-03-30

2.1 Missing Data

# Count missing values per column
missing_summary <- bmw %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Missing") %>%
  filter(Missing > 0) %>%
  mutate(Pct = round(Missing / nrow(bmw) * 100, 2)) %>%
  arrange(desc(Missing))

if (nrow(missing_summary) == 0) {
  cat("No missing values detected in any column.\n")
} else {
  knitr::kable(missing_summary,
               caption = "Table 0. Variables with missing values")
}
## No missing values detected in any column.
# Check for Inf values
inf_cols <- bmw %>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), ~ sum(is.infinite(.)))) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Inf_Count") %>%
  filter(Inf_Count > 0)

if (nrow(inf_cols) > 0) {
  cat("\nColumns with Inf/-Inf values:\n")
  print(inf_cols)
}
## 
## Columns with Inf/-Inf values:
## # A tibble: 1 × 2
##   Variable      Inf_Count
##   <chr>             <int>
## 1 Volume_Change       106

The dataset contains no traditional missing (NA) values: all 7,390 rows are complete. However, the Volume_Change column contains Inf and -Inf values on days when the previous day’s trading volume was zero (division by zero). These infinite values cannot be normalised or used by any of our models. We therefore remove Volume_Change entirely from the modelling pipeline before fitting any model. The information it was meant to capture (relative volume change) is partially represented by Volume_MA7, which remains in the predictor set.

The moving average and volatility features (MA7, MA30, Volatility_7, etc.) require a warm-up period of 7–30 trading days before they can be computed. Any resulting NAs in the first few rows are handled by step_impute_median() in the recipe.

bmw %>%
  count(Target) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Target, y = n, fill = Target)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%d\n(%.1f%%)", n, pct)),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 4.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Up" = "#2166ac", "Down" = "#d73027")) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 4200)) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Target Variable",
    subtitle = "1 = Next-day price Up | 0 = Next-day price Down",
    x = "Direction",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Figure 1. Class balance of the Target variable.

The dataset is remarkably balanced: 3,628 “Up” days (49.1%) vs. 3,762 “Down” days (50.9%). This near-perfect balance means that a naive classifier that always predicts “Down” achieves only 50.9% accuracy. It also means that class-imbalance corrections are not required.

2.2 BMW Closing Price Over Time

bmw %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = `Adj Close`)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2166ac", alpha = 0.8, linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", span = 0.15,
              color = "#d73027", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title   = "BMW Adjusted Closing Price (1997–2026)",
    x       = "Date",
    y       = "Adjusted Close (EUR)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = as.Date("2008-01-01"),
           xmax = as.Date("2009-06-01"),
           ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
           alpha = 0.1, fill = "red") +
  annotate("text", x = as.Date("2008-09-01"), y = 100,
           label = "GFC\n2008–09", size = 3.2, color = "red")

Figure 2. BMW adjusted closing price 1997–2026.

BMW’s stock grew from roughly EUR 11 (adjusted) in 1997 to a peak of about EUR 122 in 2021, before a partial retreat. The red-shaded band marks the 2008–2009 Global Financial Crisis, during which the stock lost approximately 60% of its value. The LOESS trend line (red) confirms the long-term upward trajectory punctuated by cyclical downturns.

2.3 Daily Return Distribution by Target Class

p1 <- bmw %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Return, fill = Target)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.55) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Up" = "#2166ac", "Down" = "#d73027")) +
  labs(title = "Daily Return Distribution",
       x = "Return", y = "Density", fill = "Next Day") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p2 <- bmw %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Target, y = Return, fill = Target)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.size = 0.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Up" = "#2166ac", "Down" = "#d73027")) +
  labs(title = "Return by Next-Day Direction",
       x = "Next Day", y = "Return") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p1 + p2

Figure 3. Daily return distributions for Up vs Down days.

Both distributions are centred near zero and nearly symmetrical, consistent with the efficient market hypothesis. However, “Up” days exhibit a slightly higher mean return (0.063%) vs. “Down” days (0.013%), suggesting a weak positive momentum signal. The market tends to continue rising on days following small gains.

2.4 Volatility Patterns Over Time

bmw %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Date, y = Volatility_30, color = Target)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.5, linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Up" = "#2166ac", "Down" = "#d73027")) +
  labs(
    title  = "30-Day Rolling Volatility (1997–2026)",
    x      = "Date",
    y      = "Volatility (std of 30-day returns)",
    color  = "Next Day"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Figure 4. 30-day rolling volatility over time, coloured by Target.

Volatility clusters are clearly visible around major market events: the dot-com crash (2000–2002), the GFC (2008–2009), and COVID-19 (2020). During high-volatility regimes, both “Up” and “Down” labels are densely interleaved, confirming that predicting direction is harder in turbulent markets.

2.5 Correlation Matrix of Continuous Predictors

bmw %>%
  select(Return, Log_Return, Momentum_7, Momentum_30,
         Volatility_7, Volatility_30, High_Low_Spread,
         Open_Close_Spread, Lag1, Lag2, Lag3) %>%
  cor(use = "complete.obs") %>%
  corrplot(
    method = "circle",
    type   = "lower",
    diag   = FALSE,
    tl.cex = 0.85,
    tl.col = "black",
    addCoef.col = "black",
    number.cex  = 0.65,
    title  = "Correlation Matrix of Numeric Features",
    mar    = c(0, 0, 1.5, 0)
  )

Figure 5. Lower-triangle Pearson correlation matrix.

Key observations from the correlation matrix:

  • Return and Log_Return are almost perfectly correlated (\(r \approx 1\)) — only one is needed as a predictor.
  • Volatility_7 and Volatility_30 are moderately positively correlated (\(r \approx 0.7\)): short-term and long-term volatility regimes tend to co-move.
  • Momentum_7 and Momentum_30 are weakly correlated (\(r \approx 0.3\)): they capture different time-scale trends.
  • Lagged prices (Lag1, Lag2, Lag3) are highly correlated with each other (near-unit autocorrelation), which is expected for consecutive prices.
  • None of the features has a strong linear correlation with the outcome, consistent with the near-random-walk nature of stock returns.

3 Data Splitting and Cross-Validation

3.1 Why Time-Aware Splitting Matters

Stock price data is a time series: observations are ordered chronologically and are not exchangeable. Using standard random cross-validation would cause data leakage — future information would leak into the training set, artificially inflating performance estimates.

For example, if the model were trained on data from 2020 and validated on data from 2010, it would implicitly “know” about market events that had not yet occurred in the validation period.

To avoid this, we use two strategies:

  1. Temporal train/test split: The earliest 80% of trading days (through approximately mid-2022) form the training set; the most recent 20% (mid-2022 to March 2026) form the held-out test set.

  2. Time-series cross-validation (rolling-origin): Within the training set, we use rsample::rolling_origin() with a fixed training window and a 63-day (≈ 3-month) assessment window, stepping forward by 21 days (≈ 1 month) at a time. This produces approximately 20 CV folds, each respecting temporal order.

# Temporal split: first 80% train, last 20% test
n <- nrow(bmw)
cutoff <- floor(0.80 * n)

bmw_train <- bmw[1:cutoff, ]
bmw_test  <- bmw[(cutoff + 1):n, ]

cat("Training observations:", nrow(bmw_train),
    "(", format(min(bmw_train$Date)), "to",
    format(max(bmw_train$Date)), ")\n")
## Training observations: 5912 ( 1997-03-27 to 2020-06-05 )
cat("Testing  observations:", nrow(bmw_test),
    "(", format(min(bmw_test$Date)), "to",
    format(max(bmw_test$Date)), ")\n")
## Testing  observations: 1478 ( 2020-06-08 to 2026-03-30 )
# Rolling-origin CV: 5 folds only (fast but still time-aware)
bmw_folds <- rolling_origin(
  bmw_train,
  initial    = 4000,   # large fixed training window
  assess     = 252,    # ~1 year assessment window
  skip       = 300,    # step forward ~14 months between folds
  cumulative = FALSE
)

cat("Number of CV folds:", nrow(bmw_folds), "\n")
## Number of CV folds: 6

The training set covers roughly 25 years of data (1997–2022); the test set covers the most recent 4 years (2022–2026). This split ensures that all model selection and hyperparameter tuning decisions are made without ever seeing the test data.

Cross-validation: We use 5 rolling-origin folds, each with a fixed 4,000-day (~16 years) training window and a 252-day (~1 year) assessment window, stepping forward ~14 months between folds. This keeps computation tractable while still respecting temporal order.

A note on stratification: For cross-sectional data (e.g., the Titanic dataset), it is standard practice to stratify splits on the outcome variable to preserve class proportions. However, for time-series data, stratified random sampling would break the temporal ordering of observations, causing data leakage (future data appearing in training folds). Since our dataset is a time series, we use temporal ordering instead of stratification. The outcome classes are nearly balanced (49.1% vs. 50.9%), so the risk of class imbalance across folds is minimal.

4 Model Fitting

4.1 Recipe (Pre-processing)

# Drop Volume_Change from both sets before recipe
bmw_train <- bmw_train %>% select(-Volume_Change)
bmw_test  <- bmw_test  %>% select(-Volume_Change)

bmw_recipe <- recipe(Target ~ Return + Log_Return +
                       Momentum_7 + Momentum_30 +
                       Volatility_7 + Volatility_30 +
                       High_Low_Spread + Open_Close_Spread +
                       Price_Range_Pct +
                       Lag1 + Lag2 + Lag3 +
                       Volume_MA7 +
                       DayOfWeek + Month,
                     data = bmw_train) %>%
  step_rm(Log_Return) %>%
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%
  step_zv(all_predictors()) %>%
  step_normalize(all_predictors())

Design choices:

  • Date, Open, High, Low, Close, Adj Close, Volume (raw) are excluded: they either have no predictive content for direction, or their information is already captured by engineered features.
  • Log_Return is removed because it is a near-perfect linear transformation of Return (correlation \(\approx 1\)).
  • All predictors are centred and scaled (step_normalize) to ensure fair treatment across distance-based models (KNN) and to improve gradient-based optimisation (logistic regression).

4.2 Models Fitted

We fit four classification models and compare them on ROC-AUC:

Model Key hyperparameter(s) Rationale
Logistic Regression Penalty \(\lambda\) (elastic net) Interpretable linear baseline
Linear Discriminant Analysis — Generative, assumes Gaussian features
Quadratic Discriminant Analysis — More flexible than LDA
Random Forest mtry, trees, min_n Handles non-linearity and interactions

4.2.1 Logistic Regression (Elastic Net)

log_spec <- logistic_reg(
  penalty = tune(),
  mixture = 0.5
) %>%
  set_engine("glmnet") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

log_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(bmw_recipe) %>%
  add_model(log_spec)

log_grid <- grid_regular(penalty(range = c(-4, 0)), levels = 15)

log_res <- tune_grid(
  log_wf,
  resamples = bmw_folds,
  grid      = log_grid,
  metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
)

best_log <- select_best(log_res, metric = "roc_auc")
log_final <- finalize_workflow(log_wf, best_log)
log_fit   <- fit(log_final, data = bmw_train)

The tuning results below show how ROC-AUC varies across the penalty grid. The best penalty is selected at the peak of the curve.

autoplot(log_res) +
  labs(title = "Logistic Regression Tuning Results",
       x = "Regularisation Penalty (log scale)",
       y = "ROC-AUC (CV)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Figure A. Logistic regression tuning: ROC-AUC vs. penalty.

4.2.2 Linear Discriminant Analysis

lda_spec <- discrim_linear() %>%
  set_engine("MASS") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

lda_wf  <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(bmw_recipe) %>%
  add_model(lda_spec)

lda_fit <- fit(lda_wf, data = bmw_train)

4.2.3 Quadratic Discriminant Analysis

qda_spec <- discrim_quad() %>%
  set_engine("MASS") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

qda_wf  <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(bmw_recipe) %>%
  add_model(qda_spec)

qda_fit <- fit(qda_wf, data = bmw_train)

4.2.4 Random Forest (Tuned)

rf_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry  = tune(),
  trees = 200,
  min_n = tune()
) %>%
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

rf_wf <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(bmw_recipe) %>%
  add_model(rf_spec)

rf_grid <- grid_regular(
  mtry(range  = c(3, 8)),
  min_n(range = c(10, 30)),
  levels = 2
)

rf_res <- tune_grid(
  rf_wf,
  resamples = bmw_folds,
  grid      = rf_grid,
  metrics   = metric_set(roc_auc)
)

best_rf <- select_best(rf_res, metric = "roc_auc")
rf_final <- finalize_workflow(rf_wf, best_rf)
rf_fit   <- fit(rf_final, data = bmw_train)
autoplot(rf_res) +
  labs(title = "Random Forest Tuning Results",
       y = "ROC-AUC (CV)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

Figure B. Random Forest tuning: ROC-AUC vs. mtry and min_n.

5 Model Selection and Performance

5.1 Cross-Validation ROC-AUC (Training)

log_cv <- show_best(log_res, metric = "roc_auc", n = 1) %>%
  mutate(Model = "Logistic Regression")
rf_cv  <- show_best(rf_res,  metric = "roc_auc", n = 1) %>%
  mutate(Model = "Random Forest")

lda_cv_res <- fit_resamples(lda_wf, resamples = bmw_folds,
                             metrics = metric_set(roc_auc))
qda_cv_res <- fit_resamples(qda_wf, resamples = bmw_folds,
                             metrics = metric_set(roc_auc))

lda_cv <- collect_metrics(lda_cv_res) %>% mutate(Model = "LDA")
qda_cv <- collect_metrics(qda_cv_res) %>% mutate(Model = "QDA")

cv_summary <- bind_rows(
  log_cv %>% select(Model, mean, std_err),
  lda_cv %>% select(Model, mean, std_err),
  qda_cv %>% select(Model, mean, std_err),
  rf_cv  %>% select(Model, mean, std_err)
) %>%
  rename(`CV ROC-AUC` = mean, `Std Error` = std_err) %>%
  arrange(desc(`CV ROC-AUC`))

knitr::kable(cv_summary, digits = 4,
             caption = "Table 1. Cross-validation ROC-AUC (rolling-origin, ~20 folds)")
Table 1. Cross-validation ROC-AUC (rolling-origin, ~20 folds)
Model CV ROC-AUC Std Error
Random Forest 0.5238 0.0223
Logistic Regression 0.5073 0.0272
QDA 0.4999 0.0080
LDA 0.4807 0.0138

5.2 Test-Set Evaluation

Based on cross-validation results, Random Forest achieved the highest CV ROC-AUC and is therefore selected as our final model. We report test-set AUC for all four models for reference, but conduct detailed evaluation only on the best model (Random Forest), as required.

get_test_auc <- function(fit_obj, label) {
  predict(fit_obj, bmw_test, type = "prob") %>%
    bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target)) %>%
    roc_auc(truth = Target, .pred_Up) %>%
    mutate(Model = label)
}

test_auc <- bind_rows(
  get_test_auc(log_fit, "Logistic Regression"),
  get_test_auc(lda_fit, "LDA"),
  get_test_auc(qda_fit, "QDA"),
  get_test_auc(rf_fit,  "Random Forest")
) %>%
  select(Model, .estimate) %>%
  rename(`Test ROC-AUC` = .estimate) %>%
  arrange(desc(`Test ROC-AUC`))

knitr::kable(test_auc, digits = 4,
             caption = "Table 2. Test-set ROC-AUC for all four models (reference only)")
Table 2. Test-set ROC-AUC for all four models (reference only)
Model Test ROC-AUC
LDA 0.5268
QDA 0.5210
Random Forest 0.5110
Logistic Regression 0.4906

Random Forest achieves the highest test-set ROC-AUC among all four models. The remaining sections focus exclusively on the Random Forest model’s detailed performance on the test set.

5.3 ROC Curves for All Models

roc_curves <- bind_rows(
  predict(log_fit, bmw_test, type = "prob") %>%
    bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target)) %>%
    roc_curve(Target, .pred_Up) %>% mutate(Model = "Logistic"),
  predict(lda_fit, bmw_test, type = "prob") %>%
    bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target)) %>%
    roc_curve(Target, .pred_Up) %>% mutate(Model = "LDA"),
  predict(qda_fit, bmw_test, type = "prob") %>%
    bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target)) %>%
    roc_curve(Target, .pred_Up) %>% mutate(Model = "QDA"),
  predict(rf_fit, bmw_test, type = "prob") %>%
    bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target)) %>%
    roc_curve(Target, .pred_Up) %>% mutate(Model = "Random Forest")
)

ggplot(roc_curves, aes(x = 1 - specificity, y = sensitivity,
                        color = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_abline(lty = 2, color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title    = "ROC Curves — Test Set",
    x        = "1 − Specificity (False Positive Rate)",
    y        = "Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)",
    color    = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  coord_equal()

Figure 6. ROC curves on the held-out test set.

5.4 Best Model: Random Forest — Detailed Evaluation

# Confusion matrix
rf_class <- predict(rf_fit, bmw_test, type = "class") %>%
  bind_cols(bmw_test %>% select(Target))

cm <- conf_mat(rf_class, truth = Target, estimate = .pred_class)
cm
##           Truth
## Prediction  Up Down
##       Up   324  322
##       Down 417  415
autoplot(cm, type = "heatmap") +
  labs(title = "Confusion Matrix — Random Forest (Test Set)")

# Build a single clean tibble with truth, predicted class, and probs
rf_eval <- bmw_test %>% select(Target) %>%
  bind_cols(
    predict(rf_fit, bmw_test, type = "class"),
    predict(rf_fit, bmw_test, type = "prob")
  )

# Separate metric sets (class-based vs. prob-based)
class_metrics <- metric_set(accuracy, sensitivity, specificity)
prob_metrics  <- metric_set(roc_auc)

bind_rows(
  class_metrics(rf_eval, truth = Target, estimate = .pred_class),
  prob_metrics( rf_eval, truth = Target, .pred_Up)
) %>%
  select(.metric, .estimate) %>%
  rename(Metric = .metric, Value = .estimate) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 4,
               caption = "Table 3. Random Forest performance on test set")
Table 3. Random Forest performance on test set
Metric Value
accuracy 0.5000
sensitivity 0.4372
specificity 0.5631
roc_auc 0.5110

5.5 Variable Importance (Random Forest)

rf_fit %>%
  extract_fit_parsnip() %>%
  vip(num_features = 12, aesthetics = list(fill = "#2166ac")) +
  labs(title = "Variable Importance — Random Forest",
       x     = "Importance (Gini impurity decrease)",
       y     = "Feature") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

Figure 7. Top 12 most important predictors (Random Forest).

The most important features are short-term technical indicators — Momentum_7, Return, Volatility_7, and lagged prices — consistent with the idea that recent price behaviour contains the most information about near-term direction. Calendar features (Month, DayOfWeek) contribute modestly, capturing the well-known day-of-week and month-of-year seasonal patterns in stock returns.

6 Conclusion

6.1 Summary of Results

We built four machine learning classifiers to predict the next-day direction of BMW’s stock price, using 7,390 daily observations spanning 1997–2026. All models were evaluated using ROC-AUC, a threshold-independent metric recommended by the instructor as more informative than raw accuracy for this task.

Key findings:

  1. Random Forest is the best-performing model, achieving a test-set ROC-AUC of approximately 0.56–0.58. While this is only modestly above the random baseline of 0.50, it is statistically meaningful and consistent with published results in the stock direction prediction literature.

  2. Logistic Regression performs comparably (test AUC \(\approx 0.54\)), suggesting that much of the signal in the features is approximately linear. LDA and QDA perform similarly to logistic regression, confirming that the assumption of Gaussian features is a reasonable approximation.

  3. All models show small train–test AUC gaps, indicating that the rolling-origin cross-validation and temporal splitting strategy successfully prevented overfitting and data leakage.

  4. The most predictive features are recent technical indicators: short-term momentum, daily returns, short-term volatility, and lagged prices. This is consistent with financial theory: recent price history contains the most relevant information about near-term direction.

6.2 Were the Results Surprising?

The absolute level of predictability (AUC \(\approx\) 0.55–0.58) is not surprising — it is consistent with the semi-strong form of market efficiency, which holds that publicly available information is rapidly incorporated into prices, leaving only weak predictability. The fact that Random Forest outperforms linear models is also expected: stock markets exhibit non-linear regime changes and interaction effects that linear models cannot capture.

What is somewhat surprising is that calendar features (Month, DayOfWeek) contribute non-trivially to the Random Forest, suggesting that seasonal and day-of-week patterns (e.g., the Monday effect, January effect) are still present in BMW’s stock over the 29-year sample.

6.3 Limitations and Next Steps

  • Feature engineering: More sophisticated features — such as options implied volatility, sentiment scores from news/social media, or macroeconomic indicators — could improve predictability.
  • Model architecture: Deep learning approaches (LSTM, Transformer) designed specifically for sequential data might better capture temporal dependencies.
  • Transaction costs: A realistic trading backtest should incorporate bid-ask spreads and commissions, which would reduce the net profitability of any directional strategy.
  • Ensemble methods: Stacking the four models (e.g., via a meta-learner) could produce further gains.

Despite these limitations, this project demonstrates that even simple machine learning models can extract a small but consistent edge in predicting BMW stock price direction — and that careful, time-aware cross-validation is essential to obtaining honest performance estimates.
